key_outcomes_findings.gold: Both arms improved over 12 weeks, but TAU-PLUS showed superior results (modified intention-to-treat analysis): - Primary (BDI-II): Greater symptom reduction in TAU-PLUS (-16.2 vs. -10.5 points; between-group difference: -5.7 points, p=0.03, Cohen's d=-0.73 [medium effect]). - Secondary: No significant differences in HAM-D (p=0.12) or WHO-5 (p=0.08); higher daily activity in TAU-PLUS (p=0.04, d=0.70); shorter inpatient stay (14 vs. 21 days, p=0.003); lower post-discharge re-admissions (8% vs. 24%, p=0.04) and care utilization (19% vs. 42%, p=0.01). - Other: Working alliance with Deprexis (but not therapists) predicted better MDD improvement and well-being; no adverse effects noted. Limitations: Small sample, ~20% dropout.
key_outcomes_findings.pred: At week 12, modified ITT analyses showed significant between-group differences in BDI-II scores in favor of TAU-PLUS (p=.03), corresponding to a medium effect size (d=-.73, 95% CI -1.4 to .06). Secondary outcome measures including therapist alliance were not significantly different between groups at study endpoint. The patients' working alliance with deprexis significantly predicted MDD improvement and wellbeing. TAU plus deprexis was superior to TAU in improving subjective depression severity and daily activity.

key_outcomes_findings.pred: 56 participants (48 females, mean age 45.55 ± 13.70 years) were randomized: dCBT-I (n=29) vs. digital sleep monitoring (n=27). At 8 weeks post-randomization, large treatment effects (d = 0.87–1.08) indicated robust reductions in insomnia severity in the dCBT-I arm relative to digital sleep monitoring (-3.70 points on ISI; p ≤ 0.003). At 16 weeks post-randomization, reductions were -2.97 points on ISI (p ≤ 0.003). Treatment effects in favour of dCBT-I were also found for self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables, indicating sleep improved throughout the 8-week intervention period. Linear mixed models were used to assess between-group differences. The study concluded that dCBT-I achieves clinical improvements for patients with insomnia, supporting the specific effects of CBT-I.
